The firm’s performance as a percentage of revenue is shown in the chart below:

The chart indicates gross margin compression followed by a more recent rebound, while R&D and SG&A as a percentage of revenue have fallen in the most recent reporting period, showing more recent positive financial results as revenue has grown sharply.

York’s IPO Plan

YSS has filed to sell shares of its common stock in an IPO, which will likely exceed $100 million in size, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, at least as detailed in the initial prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus.

The company is majority-owned by private equity firm AE Industrial Partners, which will have majority voting control of York immediately after the IPO.

According to York’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

The principal purposes of this offering are to increase financial flexibility, create a public market for our common stock and enable access to the public equity markets for us and our shareholders. We intend to use the net proceeds we receive from this offering for working capital to fund growth and other general corporate purposes, which may include building inventory, research and development and capital expenditures. We may also use a portion of the net proceeds to acquire or make investments in businesses, products, offerings, and technologies, although we do not have agreements or commitments for any material acquisitions or investments at this time. At this time, we have not specifically identified a large single use for which we intend to use the net proceeds and, accordingly, we are not able to allocate the net proceeds among any of these potential uses in light of the variety of factors that will impact how such net proceeds are ultimately utilized by us.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not available yet.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said that any legal exposures would not have a material adverse effect on its operations or financial condition.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Goldman Sachs, Jefferies, and Wells Fargo Securities.

York Space Is Growing Revenue Quickly On Higher Operating Losses

York is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its general growth plans, continued capabilities development and possibly future acquisitions.

The company’s financials have shown quickly growing topline revenue, growing gross profit, but fluctuating gross margin, higher operating losses and a swing to high cash used in operations.

While operating losses have been higher in absolute terms, operating margin has ‘improved’ to negative (19.1%), the best result of the past two and ¾ years.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, was negative ($29.4 million), while the company only had $22.5 million in cash and equivalents as of that date, so a cash infusion of some kind, whether via the IPO or from $140 million in available Revolving Facility credit.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have varied as revenue has increased, and its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple rose to 1.1x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will retain any future earnings for its development and growth plans.

YSS’s recent capital spending history shows it has continued to spend on capital expenditures even as it has generated negative operating cash flow.

Share-based compensation has been minimal.

The firm’s growth strategy includes seeking to leverage its PWSA system lead to gain future revenues and new DoD mission types, improve its margin structure by expanding common platform architectures, and grow recurring revenue streams via software and services.

The market opportunity for providing space communications and related technologies is very large and expected to grow at nearly10% through 2030, so the firm has strong industry opportunity dynamics in its favor.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging company status’, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders, at its discretion.

The company has revenue concentration risks in that virtually all of its revenue comes from government bid contracts, which are typically win-lose and can mean ‘lumpy’ and unpredictable revenue.

However, the firm’s revenue growth trajectory has been impressive and given the growth trajectory of the space industry, I expect institutional demand for the YSS IPO to be substantial, assuming reasonable IPO price assumptions.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more about the IPO from management.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: ETG: Strong Monthly Payer, But Not The Right Price
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

International equities have been on a strong run into 2025, and that's helped out some funds like the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund (ETG). This is a closed-end fund with a tilt of exposure outside of North America, with it leaning at roughly 45% outside that geographic region. Of course, that still leaves a lot of U.S. exposure, but making it a solid overall diversified fund—which also includes some fixed-income exposure as well as equity positions. The fund being leveraged would have also helped to see it have higher relative returns compared to the S&P 500 Index.

However, where we remain more neutral on the fund is that we've seen its discount narrow from its longer-term historical level. That's helped to boost total returns on a share price basis even further, but it can set it up for some further potential downside during the next volatile market event.

ETG Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.51
  • Discount/Premium: -5.67%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.29%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.13%
  • Leverage: 17.40%
  • Managed Assets: $2.1 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

ETG invests "primarily in global dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and seeks to distribute a high level of dividend income that qualifies for favorable federal income tax treatment."

More specifically, the investment strategy is:

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its total managed assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks of U.S. and foreign issuers that the Fund's investment adviser believes at the time of investment are eligible to pay dividends that qualify for federal income taxation at rates applicable to long-term capital gains. The Fund may invest in preferred stocks that are rated below investment grade. The Fund may also invest a portion of its assets in stocks and other securities that generate fully taxable ordinary income, including up to 30% of its total assets in securities rated below investment grade.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund will invest (i) at least 25% of its total managed assets in the securities of U.S. issuers; (ii) at least 30% of its total managed assets in securities of non-U.S. issuers, including issuers located in emerging market countries; and (iii) in issuers located in at least five different countries (including the U.S.).

The fund operates with a modest amount of leverage. Of course, even modest leverage means greater overall potential for volatility and increasing risks. They have utilized futures contracts to help hedge against the higher rate environment. Those certainly came in handy when rates were rising, but now that they've been heading lower, it has been the source of producing some losses.

On the other hand, their borrowing costs are declining, and it is beneficial to see expenses decline. When including the interest expense, the total expense ratio comes to 2.35%. That's down from fiscal 2024 at 2.57%, and the peak at 2.7% in fiscal 2023.

Performance: Discount Remains Tight

Since our last update, the fund has slightly outperformed the S&P 500 Index on a total return basis. That's positive. In fact, the fund has seen its discount widen a touch since this time, so its underlying portfolio would have performed even better. This has been since our July 2025 update.

In looking at the results on a YTD basis, compared to ETG, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and SPDR Portfolio Developed World ex-US ETF (SPDW), we see that ETG has delivered similar total share price returns as SPDW. Both are showing significantly better total return results relative to SPY—though at over 16% YTD returns, SPY is also doing incredibly well.

On the other hand, we can see below that ETG's total NAV return results fall quite a bit further relative to SPDW. Still, ETG outpaced SPY by a rather healthy margin, but the size of the lead was diminished quite materially.

This, of course, is a prime example of a result of ETG's discount/premium. It's also, to a lesser degree, the fact that the fund has a ~10% allocation to fixed-income instruments. Fixed income just hasn't performed as well relative to the equity indexes, and that's by a wide margin. So, going back to the discount/premium, on a YTD basis, we can see that the discount has narrowed sharply from a near double-digit level.

Looking back over the longer term, the fund is continuing to trade at a narrower discount relative to its historical level.

Therefore, I would continue to hold this fund, but I wouldn't look to be adding aggressively—or at all unless we get a broader market correction and/or see the discount widen to a more attractive level.

Distribution: Looking Healthy

While I wouldn't be adding to this name, the fund hasn't gone to any sort of premium yet to justify necessarily selling out of it. Further, while holding onto the position, we can look forward to the strong monthly distribution that the fund pumps out to investors. The current distribution rate works out to 7.29%, with the NAV rate being a bit more modest at 6.88%.

In terms of covering the distribution, despite owning a portfolio focused more on dividend payers and fixed-income exposure, the fund will rely heavily on capital gains to fund the shortfall between net investment income and the distribution. This is not at all unusual for a fund that is heavily invested in equity positions. The NII coverage also comes in at around 52.5%—which is actually rather high for a fund such as ETG.

ETG's Portfolio

Portfolio turnover for ETG tends to run rather high, with the latest semi-annual report coming in at 57%—and that's just for a six-month period. The five-year average between 2020 and 2024 came in at nearly 117%. Despite this, the fund doesn't necessarily see too much of a dramatic shift in its overall allocations more broadly.

That said, we have seen some allocation swings from our prior update. One of those is the geographic exposure more broadly. U.S. equities are listed at ~48%, with foreign equity at around 39%. The remainder of the portfolio is allocated to fixed income/preferred and cash.

This was previously neutralized with foreign equity at ~47% and U.S. equity around 39%. That's not necessarily the most meaningful flip, but it does suggest they started to see better value in U.S. equities compared to international.

In terms of sector weighting, we continue to see financials in the top spot—though the lead has been reduced a bit from the 31.87% allocation noted previously. Technology remains in the No. 2 spot, but has seen its percentage increase from 15.52%. Consumer discretionary comes in at the third largest allocation, which is up from the 9.23% earlier in the year. That put that sector above healthcare and industrials, which had previously been larger allocations.

Again, this isn't necessarily the most meaningful change, but ETG tends to be a more value-oriented portfolio. Seeing that the tech sleeve increased once again as we had toward the latter part of 2024, I think it is worth noting.

For the most part, this could be the result of natural market moves, which have heavily favored mega-cap names, in particular, the Magnificent 7 names. Those that are primarily classified as tech, but names like Amazon (AMZN) as a consumer discretionary and Alphabet (GOOG) are put into the communication services sector.

Along with AMZN and GOOG being present holdings in the top ten, we also have Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) from the Mag 7 list. These were actually the exact names we saw previously, in the same order, but with different percentage weightings—and that is seeing each one have its weighting increase from where it was previously.

Conclusion

ETG remains trading at a narrow discount, continuing to keep our "Hold" rating. However, it doesn't appear egregiously overvalued, and the monthly distribution looks quite healthy. That helps to make it a worthwhile candidate to keep holding over the longer term. With markets more broadly looking stretched, that is another consideration of waiting for an overall market pullback before wanting to put more capital to work in this fund. During greater equity market volatility, we can often see CEF discounts widen, which ETG would be a candidate for after narrowing to historically tight levels.

data.title: MTBA: High-Quality Agency MBS, Great Strategy, Above-Average Yield
data.description:
Spreads between mortgage rates and treasury rates reached decades-high this past year. Investing in MBS was an obvious, and broadly profitable, play in the past, and one which several funds thought to capitalize on. One of these was the Simplify MBS ETF (MTBA), which focuses on newly-issued MBS, with comparatively high coupons. Conditions were quite favorable to MTBA and MBS in the past, and I was quite bullish on the fund when I first covered it, in mid-2024.

Since then, MTBA has seen reasonably good returns, albeit slightly less than those of treasuries and bonds. Timing seems to have played a role, although I can't rule out a broadly ineffective investment strategy or active management.

Notwithstanding the above, as mortgage rates remain attractive on an absolute and relative basis and owing to MTBA's good 6.0% dividend yield, I remain bullish on the fund.

MTBA - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Holdings

Some context first.

The Federal Reserve hiked rates quite aggressively during 2022 and 2023. Although rates have come down since, rates remain broadly elevated across fixed-income markets.

Some specific assets benefitted more from these hikes than others, especially those with variable rates, or very short maturities. MBS also benefitted more than most bonds from these hikes, probably due to market movements / investor expectations (mechanically, I don't think MBS are more exposed to Fed rates than average). Spreads to treasuries more than doubled, and although these have narrowed since their 2023 highs, spreads and rates remain elevated.

MBS offered investors particularly attractive yields in the past, which boosted returns relative to other fixed-income sub-asset classes, and which seemed likely to result in outperformance. MBS have outperformed since, with benchmark MBS ETFs outperforming benchmark treasury ETFs by around 8.2% total since early 2021, around 2.0% annually. Outperformance is quite high, considering we are comparing high-quality assets with similar duration.

MTBA was specifically created to try and capitalize from the above, by focusing on newly-issued MBS with particularly attractive coupons, resulting in higher dividend yields for the fund. Newly-issued mortgages, with higher rates, will be immediately refinanced if the Fed slashes rates aggressively, so said yields are riskier than most. There is a tradeoff here, and one which might not work, but one which seemed more than reasonable to me before.

MBTA's strategy results in a 6.0% dividend yield, quite a bit higher than most fixed-income sub-asset classes including treasuries, as expected. Said figure is closest to high-yield corporate bonds, much riskier securities.

MTBA exclusively invests in agency MBS and cash. As is the case for most ETFs, it might invest a bit in other stuff here and there, but MBS and cash should generally be 100% of its portfolio.

MTBA has a duration of 3.0 years, compared to 5.3 for MBB. Higher-coupon bonds tend to have lower duration, because a greater proportion of the payments come before maturity. MTBA's MBS have higher coupons, hence its lower duration.

Finally, and as mentioned previously, MTBA's newly-issued, higher-rate mortgages will be the first to get refinanced if the Federal Reserve cuts rates. Refinancing does not mean losses, but it does mean dividend cuts, due to lower mortgage rates after refinancing, and lack of gains, MBS prices don't increase from refinancing.

Due consider that most fixed-income securities can't be refinanced in the same way, with the same conditions, so rate cuts have a different effect. The U.S. Treasury can't refinance the country's outstanding debt if rates come down, at least not until treasuries mature. If rates go down to zero, investors will almost certainly bid up the price of older treasuries with +4.0% yields. Mortgages get refinanced, so coupons or dividends get cut much quicker, and there are basically no gains if rates go down.

Due to the above, one could think of MTBA as trading some upside potential for an increased 6.0% dividend yield. Seems reasonable enough, and could deliver outperformance if rates are flat, or if mortgage spreads remain wide. I quite like MTBA's strategy and believe that current conditions are broadly favorable to it.

Performance Track-Record

MTBA's performance track-record is slightly below-average.

Absolute performance seems reasonably good, with the fund's share price marginally up since inception, total returns of a bit more than 6.0% per year. Income generated most of these returns, with the fund paying a 6.0% dividend.

On a more negative note, MTBA has slightly underperformed treasuries since inception, moderate underperformance relative to MBS indexes.

As can be seen above, most of MTBA's underperformance occurred prior to 2024, with the fund seeing significantly below-average gains at the time. Gains there were due to a sharp downwards movement in market rates, leading to higher prices across fixed-income markets. MTBA benefitted from said movement too, but by much less than average, due to the fund's below-average duration, and due to the issues with mortgage refinancing.

As mentioned previously, MBS have outperformed treasuries by quite a bit for years. Results look particularly strong since early 2021, for instance:

Recent results are worse, with MBS slightly underperforming treasuries YTD. From prior coverage, I can say that there have been other time periods of MBS underperformance. It hasn't been a smooth ride, even though looking back it seems like it was.

In my opinion, and considering the above, MTBA's performance would have been quite a bit better with somewhat better timing. Got created a bit too late to really benefit from the medium-term outperformance of MBS. Which brings me to my next point.

Overall Assessment

MTBA's strategy was meant to capitalize on wide mortgage / treasury spreads, to generate above-average returns. Said strategy has not led to outperformance in the past. In some cases, this would be enough for a hold, maybe a sell, rating from me. In MTBA's case, I decided on a hold for two reasons.

First, market conditions remain broadly attractive to MBS. Specifically, MBS rates remain at attractive and above-average levels:

As do MBS / treasury spreads.

MTBA invests in MBS, benefits from the above, and so gets a buy rating from me.

The second reason for the buy rating, is the fact that MTBA's performance track-record seems reasonable enough. Investors in MTBA have received 6.0% in income from high-quality agency MBS every year, plus a token amount of capital gains. Results are fine, and I don't think that slightly underperforming treasuries since inception warrants a change in rating. Outperformance remains the goal though, for me at least, and MTBA has failed to achieve that.

Considering the above, a buy rating seems appropriate.

Conclusion

MTBA's strong strategy, above-average 6.0% dividend yield, and favorable macro conditions, make the fund a buy. Although the fund's strategy has failed to deliver outperformance in the past, returns look reasonably good regardless, and I remain bullish about the future.

data.title: HA Sustainable Infrastructure: Structural Advantage In Their Investments
data.description:
I last wrote about HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) back in 2022 as a sell thesis because it was trading over 3X book value. Since that time, HASI's book value has improved and its market price has fallen, making valuation much more attractive today.

HASI is a wildly complex company. Not only do they invest in a large variety of assets including various forms of energy production, but they do so through VIEs (variable interest entities) and various other off-balance sheet vehicles. So even if one can discern the efficacy of the underlying assets, each of their investments has unique structure with unique waterfalls between the varying entities.

Due to financial engineering inherent in these structures, near-term cashflows are not necessarily representative of true economic expected returns.

Financial engineering is a term that often has dirty connotations, but it is not inherently a negative. It is inherently neutral. It can be used well to structure an investment to be more aligned with an investor’s goals. It can also be used deceitfully to alter the perceived expected return of a project.

So, given this vast complexity and the financial engineering inherent to structured investments, how can one get a handle on HASI’s viability as an investment?

I think there are 2 methods, which in combination, help to cut through the opacity:

  1. Look at long term financial metrics.
  2. Differentiate between advantaged return versus engineered return in complex investment vehicles.

So let us begin by taking a look at long-term charts of some of HASI’s key financial metrics and then move on to a deep dive into how they structure their complex investment vehicles.

HASI’s long-term financial metrics look good

At the most basic level we can check EBITDA to see if the business is growing. EBITDA growth is lumpy, but clearly trending up toward the right.

This metric of course is influenced by share issuance. A bigger company will have more EBITDA even if it is not more profitable on a per share basis. Many REITs grow through share issuance but have mixed track records on whether that growth is accretive on a per share basis. Indeed, HASI has been issuing quite a bit of equity.

To check if that growth was accretive on a per share basis we turn to per share metrics.

Earnings per share is just as lumpy as EBITDA, but also trending nicely upward.

Lumpiness in this sort of company can be caused by a variety of factors. 2 of the main factors that I think are causing these bumps in HASI are:

  1. Timing of depreciation schedules and recognition thereof.
  2. Variable accounting recognition of revenue from off balance sheet projects

Most of HASI’s assets are direct or indirect investment in energy infrastructure which is a steady asset class. The infrastructure produces electricity continuously and the electricity is sold through long term PPAs (power purchase agreements). Thus, the underlying revenue streams for many of HASI’s assets are smooth and it is merely the accounting elections and oddities with regard to VIEs and joint ventures that create the lumps.

As such, I would not overly react to quarterly performance and focus more on the long-term trajectory. HASI has a nice streak of dividend raises.

It is possible for a company to pay out more than they are earning, but usually that only works in the short term. The streak posted above is sufficiently long that it seems to be well supported. Further, if a company were to be paying out more than it was earning on a sustained basis, book value would erode over time.

HASI’s book value per share is climbing quite nicely.

In combination, these metrics suggest that HASI’s 5% dividend yield is fully funded and reliable.

The company level metrics serve as an indication that their projects are generally performing well. However, complex structures such as these can often obscure issues under the surface. As a given joint investment thrives or fails, different tranches will often recognize success or failure in differing magnitude and at different timings.

This is where the financial engineering of these structures makes them really difficult to judge as an outsider, especially considering that only some portion of the information is made public.

HASI’s investments come with very high ROEs. Its November presentation points to a 13.4% ROE on 2025 investments.

Energy infrastructure assets do not inherently have true returns that high.

If they did, everyone would be building them and then the competition would erode returns down to a more normal economic return.

So something about the way HASI is investing is causing them to have a much higher ROE than would be typical of normal, unstructured investment in energy infrastructure.

It behooves us as investors to ascertain whether HASI’s higher return is the result of financial engineering or true advantaged return.

High near-term returns from engineering or actual advantage?

Investments can be structured in certain ways or broken up into tranches that have different risk/return profiles.

Breaking an investment up in this way neither creates nor destroys value. One is merely engineering the financial outlook to match with differing desires of differing investors.

Most projects have a range of potential outcomes and financial engineering essentially shifts the financial impacts of those outcomes between various tranches. We can model the range of outcomes for a given project with a bell curve.

Consider a project that as a whole has a 9% expected return on invested capital. One can cut that project up into tranches with different profiles.

  • High risk tranche with 13% return but lowest priority in waterfall
  • Preferred tranche with 6% return but higher priority in waterfall

If the project performs well, there will be enough cashflow to pay both the preferred tranche and the lower tranche such that the riskier one will outperform.

However, if the project performs poorly, the lower tranche is eating the lost return while the higher tranche is still getting its 6% priority return.

Joint ventures or other sorts of investment vehicles that involve multiple parties are often catering to differing investor bases.

They can be structured in such a way that both investors are happy. The low-risk investor will take the safer yet lower return tranche while the more aggressive investor will take the high-risk / high-reward leg.

Regardless of how it is split up, the expected ROIC of the overall project is still the same. The entity is merely bucketing the return outcomes in such a way as to be more appealing to investors with differing goals and risk tolerances.

Private equity can use this financial engineering to make projects look extremely appealing. They can take a project with normal economic expected ROIC of about 8% and create a tranche for themselves where the most likely outcome is a 13% return.

It is NOT a true 13% expected return. If you run a Monte Carlo simulation of the project 1000 times, the weighted average outcome is going to be far less than 13%.

In fact, it will be the 8% of the overall project.

The tranche is merely cut out in such a way that a large portion of the outcomes will be a ~13% return. In order to get this 13% most of the time they are giving up some of the extreme upside scenarios to other tranches and probably taking on the risk of some of the extreme downside scenarios.

The project gets marketed to investors in the private equity as a 13% return project. That is the dirty usage of financial engineering. It can give projects the appearance of being far better than they are in proper analysis of forward expected outcomes.

Again, this doesn’t necessarily make it a bad investment. The risky tranche still has a scenario weighted average return that approximates a normal return. It just might be disappointing to investors who went in thinking that the promised 13% was the scenario weighted average.

Bringing this discussion back to HASI, we want to find out if their very high ROE is the result of financial engineering or rather reflective of underlying advantage that allows them to somehow capture a higher ROE.

Separating economic advantage from financial engineering

HASI has been operating in the sustainable energy space for a long time and has developed quite a bit of expertise. I see this experience more as being required to play in the space rather than a source of outperformance.

Other companies building energy infrastructure, such as the electric utilities, have also been doing it for a very long time and also have access to top notch engineers.

Thus, I think HASI is fully capable of competently overseeing a large solar + storage project, but it is not a differentiator. In its 3Q25 earnings, HASI announced a new $1.2B investment.

“New $1.2 billion investment in a 2.6 GW utility-scale renewable project closed in October.”

This project is a joint venture with KKR and is to be managed through its CCH1 vehicle.

HASI is the manager of CCH1 per the language in their 10-Q (bold added for clarity):

“In addition, HASI, through its indirect subsidiaries, HASI Securities, LLC (the “Broker-Dealer”) and CarbonCount Holdings Manager LLC (the “Asset Manager”, and, together with the Broker-Dealer, the “Service Providers”), is engaged by CCH1 pursuant to a services agreement (the “Services Agreement”) to provide certain services to CCH1.”

In exchange for providing management and broker dealer services HASI collects a fee stream. Again per the 10-Q, they get 1% on transactions and another 0.5%-1% on invested capital.

“CCH1 pays the Broker-Dealer, for provision of the services provided, an upfront fee on each funding of investments generally equal to 1% of the total cash consideration funded by CCH1 to the applicable investment counterparty. CCH1 also pays the Asset Manager, for provision of the services provided by it, ongoing fees in respect of asset management and administering the management and operation of CCH1, payable when deducted from CCH1’s cash available for distribution. The fee payable to the Asset Manager is calculated on the basis of certain performance thresholds and will generally not be less than 0.5% of invested capital per annum, subject to certain limited exceptions, nor more than 1.00%.”

This is a game changer.

The underlying project, I suspect, will have a normal economic return for an energy infrastructure project.

HASI, however, will be getting an advantaged portion of the return. Their equity investment in the venture will get the normal economic return, but the management fee stream is bonus return added on top.

As the project gets invested and levered up, much of HASI’s equity can be pulled out such that their remaining equity exposure is relatively small compared to the fee stream.

CCH1 as a vehicle can have more assets added into it. Its fee stream is growing.

At $22 million annual fees, it is generating $0.17 per share in income for HASI.

This income is additional return on top of the normal economic return.

Based on the management fee stream, HASI is getting a significantly higher true ROE than a normal project would produce. I think that bodes well for long-term returns.

Wrapping it up

HASI is extremely difficult to analyze given the complexity of their investment structures. Given healthy long-term financial metrics and the embedded advantage of the fee streams, I am inclined to be bullish.
